title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Ghost in the Machine: Searching for Dark Matter
content.text: Every star, planet, and galaxy we can see makes up only about 5% of the universe. The rest is invisible, a mysterious substance that scientists call 'dark matter.' We cannot see it, touch it, or detect it with traditional telescopes, yet we know it is there because of the gravitational pull it exerts on visible matter. || (41) || [41] This invisible 'glue' is what prevents galaxies from flying apart [/41]. If only visible matter existed, the stars at the edges of galaxies would move much slower than they actually do. Something else—something massive and unseen—must be providing the extra gravity needed to hold them together. || (42) || [42] The search for this elusive substance has become the greatest detective story in modern physics [/42]. Since it doesn't emit or reflect light, researchers have had to get creative. One approach involves looking for 'WIMPs' (Weakly Interacting Massive Particles), which are thought to pass through normal matter without leaving a trace. || (43) || [43] To catch these 'ghost' particles, scientists have built massive detectors deep underground [/43]. By placing these experiments in old gold mines or under mountains, they hope to shield them from the 'noise' of cosmic rays that constantly bombard the Earth’s surface. || (44) || [44] So far, however, these detectors have remained stubbornly silent [/44]. Despite years of searching, no one has yet made a direct detection of a dark matter particle. This has led some physicists to wonder if we are looking for the wrong thing entirely. || (45) || [45] Perhaps the problem isn't an unseen particle, but our understanding of gravity itself [/45]. A theory known as MOND (Modified Newtonian Dynamics) suggests that at very large scales, the laws of gravity behave differently than they do in our solar system. || (46) || [46] While MOND has some success, it struggles to explain other cosmic phenomena [/46]. Most cosmologists still believe that dark matter is a physical substance, even if it remains hidden. As our technology improves, the race to find the first 'spark' in the dark continues, promising to rewrite our understanding of reality.

paragraphs.A: [42] It was in the 1970s that astronomer Vera Rubin first provided the evidence for this discrepancy [/42]. Her observations of galactic rotation speeds forced the scientific community to accept that the universe was far more crowded than it appeared.
paragraphs.B: [41] This effect can be seen throughout the cosmos, acting as a structural framework for everything we see [/41]. Without it, the large-scale structures of the universe, like galaxy clusters, would never have formed in the first place.
paragraphs.C: [44] This lack of results is not due to a lack of effort or ingenuity [/44]. The experiments are some of the most sensitive ever built, capable of detecting the tiny amount of heat generated if a single WIMP were to strike an atom.
paragraphs.D: [43] The idea is that in the absolute stillness of these subterranean labs, a WIMP might occasionally bump into a nucleus of xenon or germanium [/43]. This rare collision would produce a flash of light or a vibration that scientists could measure.
paragraphs.E: [46] This alternative view remains controversial in the scientific community [/46]. It would require us to abandon Einstein's General Relativity, a theory that has passed every other test we have thrown at it for over a century.
paragraphs.F: [45] This shift in perspective would represent a fundamental change in our map of the universe [/45]. It would mean that the 'extra' gravity we observe is not caused by missing matter, but by a flaw in our equations.
paragraphs.G: Dark energy is another mystery, even more powerful than dark matter. While dark matter pulls things together, dark energy is pushing the universe apart at an accelerating rate.

questions.explanation: B expande la idea del 'pegamento' invisible, mencionando que actúa como un marco estructural para el cosmos.
A introduce la evidencia histórica de Vera Rubin sobre la discrepancia en la rotación de las galaxias.
D explica qué se espera que pase dentro de los detectores subterráneos (choque de partículas), conectando con el párrafo anterior.
C matiza el hecho de que no se hayan encontrado resultados, aclarando que no es por falta de sensibilidad en los equipos.
F conecta con la idea de que quizás la gravedad sea la clave, explicando que el fallo estaría en las ecuaciones y no en materia faltante.
E menciona que esta visión alternativa obligaría a abandonar la Relatividad General de Einstein, justificando por qué es controversial.
